_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Charles County, MD. One of the oldest continuous court record series in America — records from 1658, the original county formation year. Charles County's records survived without major catastrophic loss, making it one of the richest genealogical sources on the Western Shore.

current_courthouse.location: La Plata, MD — 200 Charles Street (county seat moved from Port Tobacco to La Plata in 1895 when the railroad bypassed Port Tobacco)
records_housed: court records post-1895 at La Plata
historical records at Maryland State Archives

previous_courthouses.location: Port Tobacco — early courts met at various locations in the Port Tobacco area
previous_courthouses.fate: No permanent structure survived. Records maintained by the county clerk.

previous_courthouses.location: Port Tobacco — brick courthouse constructed circa 1727, rebuilt after fire in 1892
previous_courthouses.fate: The 1892 fire at the Port Tobacco courthouse damaged some records but was not catastrophic. The county seat moved to La Plata in 1895 after the railroad bypassed Port Tobacco.

record_losses.cause: Fire at the Port Tobacco courthouse
records_destroyed: some loose papers and estate files
scattered court records from the most recent decades
records_survived: records from the colonial period largely survived because the bound volumes were more resistant to fire damage
record_losses.description: The 1892 fire at the Port Tobacco courthouse damaged some records but was far less destructive than the Prince George's County fire of 1882. Most bound volumes — deed books, will books, court minutes — survived. The loss was concentrated in loose papers, recent estate files, and unbound documents. The move to La Plata in 1895 also involved some record dispersal, but the critical bound volumes were transferred intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The Charles County courthouse fire of 1892 was a narrow escape — the bound volumes that contain {ancestor_name}'s deed or will likely survived, but the loose papers — the letters, the receipts, the unbound documents that add texture to a life — may have been among the losses.
record_losses.source: Newman, Harry Wright. Charles County Gentry. Washington, DC: Privately published, 1940.

record_losses.cause: County seat move from Port Tobacco to La Plata
records_destroyed: none deliberately destroyed, but some dispersal and disorganization occurred during the move
records_survived: bound volumes transferred intact to La Plata
record_losses.description: The county seat moved from Port Tobacco to La Plata in 1895 because the railroad had bypassed Port Tobacco. The physical move of records involved some risk, but the critical volumes survived.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The county seat moved because Port Tobacco died — the creek silted shut, the railroad went to La Plata, and {ancestor_name}'s records followed the courthouse from the old port town to the new railroad town.

deeds.completeness: Very good. Charles County deed records survive from 1658 — among the oldest continuous county land record series in America. The bound volumes weathered the 1892 fire and the 1895 county seat move.
deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest decades (1658–1670s). Post-1680 records are essentially complete.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis; Charles County Circuit Court, La Plata
deeds.online_access: Maryland State Archives (mdlandrec.net); FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com

wills_estates.completeness: Very good. Will books survive from the 1660s. Colonial-era wills also filed with the Prerogative Court at the State Archives. Inventories are exceptionally detailed for the colonial period.
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers lost in the 1892 fire. Bound will books survived.
wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives; Charles County Register of Wills

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1777. Earlier marriages tracked through church records — both Catholic (Jesuit missions) and Anglican (Durham and William & Mary parishes).
marriages.gaps: No marriage licenses before 1777. Church records are the primary substitute.
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; Charles County Clerk of Court

court_minutes.completeness: Very good. Court proceedings survive from 1658. These early court records document indentured servitude, land disputes, criminal cases, and the transition from indentured to enslaved labor — essential for understanding the county's social history.
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; some FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Maryland statewide registration began 1898. Compliance improved through the 1910s.
vital_records.repository: Maryland Department of Health; Maryland State Archives

church_records.parish: St. Thomas Manor (Jesuit/Catholic, est. 1640s); Durham Parish (Anglican, est. 1692); William & Mary Parish (Anglican, est. 1692)
church_records.completeness: The Jesuit records from St. Thomas Manor and Newtown Manor are among the oldest continuous Catholic records in English America. They document baptisms, marriages, burials, and plantation operations including enslaved workers. Anglican parish vestry records from 1692.
church_records.repository: Georgetown University Library (Jesuit records); Maryland Province Archives of the Society of Jesus; Maryland State Archives (Anglican records); Maryland Diocesan Archives
church_records.online_access: Georgetown University digital collections; published transcriptions; FamilySearch

substitute_sources.source_type: Jesuit mission records — St. Thomas Manor, Newtown Manor
substitute_sources.description: The Jesuit missions in Charles County maintained records from the 1640s that document Catholic families, enslaved workers on the Jesuit plantations, and the broader Catholic community. These records include sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials), plantation account books, and correspondence. The Jesuit records are the oldest continuous institutional records in Maryland.
substitute_sources.repository: Georgetown University Library, Special Collections; Maryland Province Archives of the Society of Jesus
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Catholic families in Charles County. The Jesuit plantation records also document enslaved workers by name — critical for African American genealogy in southern Maryland. The sacramental registers cover families that may not appear in Anglican parish records.
substitute_sources.source: Curran, Robert Emmett. The Bicentennial History of Georgetown University. Washington, DC: Georgetown University Press, 1993.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Charles County enumerated from the first federal census. Slave schedules (1850–1860) identify slaveholders.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. Charles County's large enslaved population makes the slave schedules particularly important.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Charles County, MD.

substitute_sources.description: The federal investigation of Lincoln's assassination generated extensive records documenting Charles County residents — witness statements, military tribunal testimony, and correspondence. These records name specific individuals, describe their properties, and document their movements during April 1865.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; Library of Congress; Surratt House Museum archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: If an ancestor was in Charles County in April 1865, they may appear in the assassination investigation records — as a witness, a suspect, or a neighbor whose statement was recorded by military investigators.
substitute_sources.source: Steers, Edward, Jr. Blood on the Moon. University Press of Kentucky, 2001.

narrative_hooks: Charles County's records reach back to 1658 — among the oldest in English America. If {ancestor_name} was in the county early enough, the paper trail stretches back nearly four centuries.

The Jesuit records at St. Thomas Manor document Catholic families and enslaved workers from the 1640s — if {ancestor_name} was Catholic in Charles County, the mission records may hold baptisms, marriages, and burials that the courthouse never recorded.

Port Tobacco was the county seat until the creek silted shut and the railroad went elsewhere — {ancestor_name}'s records were filed at a courthouse in a dying town, then moved to La Plata when the county followed the railroad.

The Lincoln assassination investigation swept through Charles County in April 1865 — federal investigators recorded the name, property, and movements of every person along Booth's escape route, and {ancestor_name} may be in those files.
source_references: Newman, Harry Wright. Charles County Gentry. Washington, DC: Privately published, 1940.
Curran, Robert Emmett. The Bicentennial History of Georgetown University. Washington, DC: Georgetown University Press, 1993.
Steers, Edward, Jr. Blood on the Moon: The Assassination of Abraham Lincoln. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2001.
Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
